prompt:
You are extracting atomic facts from a topic document.

The topic document below contains all source text for one specific topic, assembled with provenance headings. Your output must pass all Five Tests.

**Test 1 — Stand-Alone**: A reader with no other facts must fully understand the policy. No implied "who does this apply to?", "when does it apply?", "except what?". Include the subject explicitly in every fact.

**Test 2 — One Topic**: A single noun phrase names the subject. If "and" is needed to name the subject, split into two facts.

**Test 3 — Reconstruction**: Concatenating all your facts must be informationally equivalent to the topic document: same obligations, permissions, exceptions, numerical values. No information omitted, none added.

**Test 4 — Canonical Form**: Active voice. No document-navigation language ("as mentioned in", "refer to", "see above", "this section"). No redundant preamble ("it is important to note that", "this policy states", "please be aware").

**Test 5 — Template-Slot Ready**: No audience-specific framing baked in ("as a parent, you should…", "staff are reminded that…"). No channel-specific framing ("as you read this handbook…").

Instructions:
- This is the only pass that writes content — rewrite source text into canonical form
- Eliminate redundancy across provenance spans (same obligation stated in multiple spans → one fact)
- Split only at true topic boundaries (when the subject noun phrase changes)
- `title`: short noun phrase naming the subject of this fact
- `kind`: assign ONLY for clearly non-policy content — 'rationale', 'principle', or 'context'. Leave null for all policy facts (rules, procedures, fees, contact details, definitions)
- `topic_tags`: kebab-case tags, consistent with the topic_slug used in grouping

Audience scope: parents
Channel scope: parent-handbook

Topic document:
### School Hours and Attendance > School Hours, Punctuality and Drop-Off / Pick-Up

**The Importance of Punctuality**
We encourage families to arrive before 9:00 a.m., as:

*   Guides personally greet each student between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., creating a welcoming atmosphere that encourages students to begin their activities proactively.
*   Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. disrupt the flow, as students who arrived on time have already started their activities, and guides are engaged in presenting materials. This may cause a late-arriving student to feel disoriented or unsettled.
*   Punctuality fosters a focused and productive environment, supporting students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility, while building positive habits for their future growth.
raw_response: {"facts":[{"title":"Suggested arrival time for students","content":"Families are encouraged to arrive at school before 9:00 a.m. because guides personally greet students between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., fostering a welcoming atmosphere.","kind":null,"topic_tags":["school-hours","attendance","punctuality"]},{"title":"Impact of late arrivals on students","content":"Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. can disrupt the flow of activities, as students who arrived earlier have already begun their work and guides are engaged in presenting materials, potentially causing late-arriving students to feel disoriented or unsettled.","kind":null,"topic_tags":["school-hours","attendance","punctuality"]},{"title":"Benefits of punctuality for students","content":"Punctuality promotes a focused and productive environment, which supports students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility while building positive habits for future growth.","kind":"principle","topic_tags":["school-hours","attendance","punctuality"]}]}

facts.title: Suggested arrival time for students
facts.content: Families are encouraged to arrive at school before 9:00 a.m. because guides personally greet students between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., fostering a welcoming atmosphere.

facts.title: Impact of late arrivals on students
facts.content: Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. can disrupt the flow of activities, as students who arrived earlier have already begun their work and guides are engaged in presenting materials, potentially causing late-arriving students to feel disoriented or unsettled.

facts.title: Benefits of punctuality for students
facts.content: Punctuality promotes a focused and productive environment, which supports students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility while building positive habits for future growth.
